briefing.opening: Good morning. I will now begin the climate briefing for June 9, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Escalating Climate Crisis, Global Alarm Bells Ringing
sections.content: Several reports released today highlight the direct impacts of the climate crisis on various aspects of our lives and the deepening concerns surrounding them. In the United States, pessimism regarding climate crisis response is spreading, with 62% of people believing that efforts to address the crisis will fail [1, 9]. Extreme weather events are already causing fluctuations in seafood prices [4], and ecosystem destruction is becoming visible, such as a special exhibition on endangered species due to the climate crisis being held at Seomjin River [6]. Internationally, the severe impacts of climate change on human health and social systems are being reported, including changes in Biscayne Bay [42], links to child stunting and regional inequality [43], and threats to global blood supplies [46]. Specifically, Europe is being reshaped by Arctic heatwaves and large-scale wildfires [47], while in Australia, warnings have emerged that climate change is already making residents poorer and causing tens of thousands of dollars in annual losses [48, 59]. Furthermore, the 'super' El Niño phenomenon is exacerbating an unequal global economy, leading to life-threatening water shortages due to drought in Bangladesh [64, 65].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Domestic Carbon Neutrality Transition: Where Policy Meets Technology
sections.content: Amidst this global crisis, multifaceted efforts are actively underway domestically to transition towards a carbon-neutral society. The National Institute of Ecology is contributing to awareness-raising by publishing 'Climate Change, Carbon Neutrality, and Ecological Stories' [2], while Sokcho City and various local governments are encouraging citizen participation through the operation of 'Visiting Carbon Neutrality Schools' and practical education programs [5, 18, 26, 27, 30]. In the industrial sector, LS MnM is addressing climate change as a material issue through its sustainability report [8], and the transition to low-carbon steel and carbon neutrality is gaining momentum with the enactment of the special enforcement decree for the steel industry [16], marking the beginning of changes in key industries. Furthermore, the search for technological solutions is also active, including Wando County securing national funding for smart aquaculture projects [7], LG Electronics promoting the application of blue carbon marine forests [11], and emphasizing timber utilization, cement innovation, and the introduction of CCUS technology in the construction sector [10, 14]. The leading role of local governments is also noteworthy, with the Gwangmyeong mayor-elect's commitment to achieving sustainable carbon neutrality [12] and Jangsu County exceeding its greenhouse gas reduction target by 114% [23].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Action and Electrification Goals
sections.content: The international community is also accelerating action by establishing concrete goals and strategies to address the climate crisis. The COP31 Presidency has announced a global target focused on electrification, proposing to expand the share of electrification to 35% by 2035 [3, 44, 63]. Boulder, USA, has updated its climate action plan, focusing on resilience and accelerated emissions reductions [45], and the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC) encouraged private sector participation by delivering an opening statement for the business and industry sectors at this climate change conference [50]. Furthermore, countries are strengthening climate adaptation efforts in infrastructure and public health sectors, including the development of national documents on health and climate change strategies [55] and highway authorities issuing new guidelines to adapt roads to climate change [56]. A report indicating a slight decrease in Scotland's greenhouse gas emissions but a slowdown in the pace of progress [58, 61] suggests that continuous efforts are needed.

sections.title: 🌱 Renewable Energy Expansion: Investment and Challenges
sections.content: In the renewable energy sector, a key driver in responding to the climate crisis, investments and business expansions are actively taking place both domestically and internationally. As Korea enters the era of 100GW renewable energy, discussions are being raised about the role of pumped-hydro storage [31]. Furthermore, DASCO is establishing a bridgehead for 'K-Renewable Energy' to advance overseas, pursuing a 1GW solar power project in the Philippines and expecting an export effect of 1 trillion won [32, 39]. Domestic companies continue to make large-scale investments and expand their businesses, with Taihan Electric Wire securing the Haenam solar power grid project [33], and SK and KKR signing a 1.8 trillion won renewable energy big deal [35]. Incheon City's expansion of the resident-participatory 'Sunlight Income Village' project [36] and DS Investment & Securities' forecast of a solar installation boom [37] show positive signals for the widespread adoption of renewable energy. Moreover, as the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD) invested 175 million euros in wind and solar projects in Southeast Europe, opening up cooperation opportunities for Korean renewable energy companies [41], it is crucial to accelerate the renewable energy transition through international cooperation and investment.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the escalating climate crisis, alongside the multifaceted domestic and international efforts to overcome it and the critical importance of transitioning to renewable energy. This is a crucial time for sustained interest and participation in climate action. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: ‘Woefully unprepared’: extreme heat will double US hospitalizations by 2040, study finds
articles.description: Sharp rise in hospital visits will in turn drive up annual healthcare costs for heat-related conditions to over $1bnPeople in the US are poised to endure another summer of unusually ferocious heat and there will be little respite in the years ahead, with a new study finding that the coming 15 years could see a doubling in hospitalizations due to heat-related illnesses.The number of annual heat-related emergency department visits or hospitalizations across the US are set to rise from about 109,00

summary.phenomenon: In the U.S., annual hospitalizations and emergency room visits due to heat-related illnesses are projected to double from the current approximately 109,000 cases by 2040.
summary.outlook: A sharp rise in hospital visits due to heat-related illnesses will increase annual healthcare costs to over $1 billion, and extreme heat will continue in the coming years.

articles.original_description: Sharp rise in hospital visits will in turn drive up annual healthcare costs for heat-related conditions to over $1bnPeople in the US are poised to endure another summer of unusually ferocious heat and there will be little respite in the years ahead, with a new study finding that the coming 15 years could see a doubling in hospitalizations due to heat-related illnesses.The number of annual heat-related emergency department visits or hospitalizations across the US are set to rise from about 109,00

articles.title: ‘Electrify daily life’, urges Cop31 host
articles.description: Third of world’s energy needs should come from electricity by 2035, says Murat Kurum, as priorities set out for this year’s UN climate summitThe world should aim to meet a third of its energy needs from electricity within a decade to cut greenhouse gas emissions, the host of the next UN climate summit has said.While about a third of global electricity generation already comes from renewable sources, other energy-intensive sectors – chiefly transport, heating and industries – have lagged behind.

summary.phenomenon: Currently, about one-third of global electricity generation comes from renewable sources, but the electrification of energy-intensive sectors such as transport, heating, and industry lags behind. The host of COP31 urged that one-third of the world's energy needs be met by electricity by 2035.

articles.original_description: Third of world’s energy needs should come from electricity by 2035, says Murat Kurum, as priorities set out for this year’s UN climate summitThe world should aim to meet a third of its energy needs from electricity within a decade to cut greenhouse gas emissions, the host of the next UN climate summit has said.While about a third of global electricity generation already comes from renewable sources, other energy-intensive sectors – chiefly transport, heating and industries – have lagged behind.

articles.description: The arid Barind region was transformed by aquifer wells but now the water system is collapsing under the pressure of the climate crisis and decades of extractionIn the parched fields of north-west Bangladesh, where the earth hardens into cracked red clay beneath an unforgiving sun, farmers in the Barind region say they are watching the foundations of rural life disappear underground.For decades, groundwater transformed Barind – one of Bangladesh’s driest regions – into a productive agricultural

summary.phenomenon: Farmers in Bangladesh's northwestern Barind region are witnessing the foundations of rural life disappear as wells dry up and the groundwater system collapses due to drought.
summary.cause: Due to the climate crisis and decades of groundwater extraction.
summary.outlook: Farmers fear that people might wage war over water scarcity.
